event: Lower Paleolithic stone tools, including Acheulean hand-axes, indicate early hominin presence in the region of present-day Senegal.
Neolithic communities along the Senegal River and Atlantic coast develop fishing, pottery, and early settled life.
Coastal populations build extensive shell middens (kitchen middens) along the Saloum Delta, reflecting long-term maritime subsistence.
Megalithic builders begin erecting the laterite stone circles of the Senegambia region, later a UNESCO World Heritage site.
Iron-working and agricultural societies spread across the Senegal River valley, integrating into trans-Saharan trade routes.
The Kingdom of Takrur emerges along the lower Senegal River as a powerful state trading gold, salt, and slaves across the Sahara.
Takrur's rulers and many of its people adopt Islam, making it one of the earliest Islamic states in West Africa.
War-Jabi, ruler of Takrur, is recorded as a Muslim, and the kingdom becomes a center of Islamic learning and the Almoravid movement's early support.
The eastern parts of present-day Senegal fall under the expanding Mali Empire following Sundiata Keita's rise.
The Wolof Empire of Jolof is founded, uniting several states across the central and northern regions under a single buurba (king).
Portuguese navigators led by figures such as Dinis Dias reach the Senegalese coast and the Cap-Vert peninsula.
Portuguese mariners explore the Senegal River and establish early trade contacts for gold, ivory, and enslaved people.
A Jolof prince, Bumi Jeleen, travels to Portugal and is baptized, illustrating early Euro-African diplomatic and religious ties.
The Jolof Empire fragments after the Battle of Danki, as the coastal states of Kayor, Baol, Walo, and Sine-Saloum assert independence.
Dutch traders begin operating along the Senegalese coast, intensifying European competition for the Atlantic trade.
The Dutch establish a fortified trading post on the island of Gorée, which becomes a hub of the Atlantic slave trade.
France founds the trading settlement of Saint-Louis at the mouth of the Senegal River, its first permanent base in West Africa.
France captures Gorée Island from the Dutch, consolidating French dominance over the region's coastal trade.
The Torodbe-led Islamic revolution in Fouta Toro overthrows the ruling dynasty, establishing a Muslim theocratic state along the river.
The Congress of Vienna confirms French possession of Senegal's coastal posts after the Napoleonic Wars.
France abolishes slavery in its colonies, and inhabitants of Senegal's communes gain French citizenship rights.
Louis Faidherbe becomes governor and launches military campaigns and infrastructure projects that expand French control inland.
Faidherbe founds Dakar on the Cap-Vert peninsula, establishing a settlement that will become the colonial and national capital.
El Hadj Umar Tall, the Tukulor jihad leader from Fouta Toro, dies, ending a major Islamic state-building movement that resisted French expansion.
Lat Dior, the damel of Kayor, is killed resisting French railway construction, marking the effective end of indigenous kingdom resistance.
Senegal becomes part of French West Africa (Afrique Occidentale Française), with Saint-Louis as the federation's capital.
The capital of French West Africa is transferred from Saint-Louis to Dakar.
Blaise Diagne is elected as the first African deputy to the French National Assembly, championing African rights within the empire.
Blaise Diagne secures full French citizenship for residents of Senegal's Four Communes in exchange for military recruitment.
African soldiers (tirailleurs) are massacred at the Thiaroye camp near Dakar after protesting unpaid wages, a defining colonial trauma.
Senegal becomes a French overseas territory, and Léopold Sédar Senghor is elected to the French Constituent Assembly.
Senegal votes to become an autonomous republic within the French Community under the new constitution of the Fifth Republic.
Senegal joins French Sudan (Mali) to form the short-lived Mali Federation.
Senegal achieves full independence on 20 August after the breakup of the Mali Federation, with Léopold Sédar Senghor as its first president.
Senegal is admitted as a member state of the United Nations on 28 September.
Prime Minister Mamadou Dia is arrested in an alleged coup attempt, cementing Senghor's presidential dominance.
A new constitution establishes a strong presidential system, and Senegal becomes a founding member of the Organisation of African Unity.
Senghor introduces a limited multiparty system, gradually opening Senegal's political landscape.
Léopold Sédar Senghor voluntarily steps down, becoming one of the first African leaders to relinquish power peacefully.
Abdou Diouf succeeds Senghor as president and lifts remaining restrictions on political parties.
Senegal and The Gambia form the Senegambia Confederation in an attempt to integrate the two neighboring states.
The separatist Movement of Democratic Forces of Casamance (MFDC) launches an insurgency in the southern Casamance region.
Violent ethnic clashes erupt with Mauritania over the Senegal River border, displacing tens of thousands and severing diplomatic ties.
The Senegambia Confederation is dissolved after The Gambia resists deeper integration.
The CFA franc is devalued by 50 percent, causing economic hardship but boosting Senegal's export competitiveness.
Abdoulaye Wade wins the presidency, ending four decades of Socialist Party rule in Senegal's first democratic transfer of power.
The ferry MV Le Joola capsizes off the Gambian coast, killing nearly 2,000 people in one of the worst peacetime maritime disasters.
Macky Sall defeats incumbent Abdoulaye Wade in a runoff election after disputes over Wade's bid for a third term.
Senegal hosts and contributes to international responses to the West African Ebola outbreak while containing its own single case.
Deadly nationwide protests erupt after the arrest of opposition leader Ousmane Sonko, exposing deep political tensions.
Bassirou Diomaye Faye wins the presidency days after being released from prison, becoming Senegal's youngest president in a landmark election.
